text: We began by matching virtual bronchoscopy to real-time bronchoscopy.
As the bronchoscope is guided into the peripheral airways, shape sensing technology identifies exactly how far the bronchoscope has traveled in the distal airways.
Once the computer has enough information to detect the presence within a specific lobe, the lobe is illuminated, as seen on the bottom left screen.
We then begin navigation.
Virtual bronchoscopy shows the customized path in the bottom left corner.
Real-time bronchoscopy is seen in the bottom right.
There's constant feedback in terms of the distance, the location, and the plural boundaries, as is denoted by the anatomy border.
In this case, an endoluminal aspect of the lesion is seen during real-time bronchoscopy, which confirms the location of the bronchoscope.
Once the bronchoscopist is within close proximity to the target, radial EBUS is used to detect an appropriate signal coinciding with the lesion, as seen in the bottom left screen.
Once biopsies are taken, the specimens are read with rapid pathologic evaluation on site to confirm sampling of the nodule in question,
rather than sampling of benign lung parenchyma around the lesion.
Once adequate tissue samples are obtained, the optical probe is then inserted once again through the catheter to confirm adequate hemostasis.
The robotic bronchoscope is withdrawn and undocked from the patient, and standard EBIS is performed for mediastinal staging.
Robotic resection was performed.
On initial exploration, there was no evidence of disseminated pleural disease or abnormal pleural fluid.
The inferior pulmonary ligament was identified and exposed.
Using the fenestrated graspers and the bipolar dissector, the inferior pulmonary ligament was divided.
As lymph nodes were exposed in stations 8 and 9R, they were removed with the bipolar dissector and sent for intraoperative frozen pathology examination.
The right inferior pulmonary vein was dissected free and left intact to prevent lobar congestion.
We identified the right lower lobe segmental pulmonary arteries.
We were able to identify the middle lobe artery as well as two large basilar segmental branches.
The middle lobe branch appeared to be coming off the basilar segmental branches.
Dissection and stapling of the individual branches of the basilar segments was performed.
After taking a branch of the pulmonary artery, the inferior vein was divided.
The remaining basilar branches were divided.
The fissure was opened further posteriorly to expose the superior segmental artery.
After adequate exposure, the superior segmental artery was stapled and divided.
The anterior fissure was open to identify the bronchus
The bronchus was dissected clearly to identify the RC2 bronchus and the hilar nodes in this area that were independently removed when necessary.
The lower lobe was rotated and the bronchus was isolated.
The right lung was inflated to ensure that the right middle lobe bronchus was preserved.
The bronchus was divided and the posterior parenchymal fissure was divided to complete the lobectomy.
We performed lymphadenectomy in stations 10R, 7, and 4R.
These nodes were sent for frozen pathology.
Using an endoscopic specimen bag, the right lower lobe was removed through the subxiphoid incision.
We have found that this allows for removal of bigger specimens and the patients appear to have less post-operative pain.
Intercostal nerve blocks were then performed for postoperative pain control using liposomal bupivacaine.
The patient tolerated the procedure well.
